description: A14FJ - Day 5, part 4: Kitty Hawk begins Solo Operations

tokens: Apollo 14's LM Antares has touched down on Fra Mauro, and now Stu Roosa is orbiting the Moon on his own, about to press on with a busy schedule of observations as per his Solo Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Some of the numbers associated with the landing.
The ignition time for power descent initiation was 108:42:29 Ground Elapsed Time.
Actual touchdown was at 108:55:14, a total time in power descent of 12 minutes 46 seconds.

At the present time the command service module, Kitty Hawk, is on air-to-ground 2 which will be recorded in the news room and transcribed, however, the air-to-ground 1 for Lunar Module, Antares on the lunar surface, is prime and will be carried live as the conversations continue.
When you have loss of signal with the Command Module in 16 minutes.
We're up live with Shepard and Mitchell in Antares at the Fra Mauro landing site.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've got you on a different channel now than the LM.
For some reason, we're getting a little bit of noise out of your downlink.
But did you relay everything that was going on - or hear everything that was going on?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: They really put her in there.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Hey, Ron, let me know when I can talk without disrupting things.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: It's all quieted down, and your downlink's on another channel; so, no problems.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Hey, that LTC camera's still acting up the same way; I tried both nonessential Bus positions, and I cycled all the connectors - could see no visi - visible damage to the pins.
I did look at that slit in the shutter, and the first two times I actuated it, it stayed pretty much over 1 inch from the edge.
And then, the next couple of times, it stopped about 2 inches from the edge -one each time a different edge, but out about 2 inches, maybe just a little more.
It still has the clacking sound; it still wants to - intermittently fire off a frame in standby, even with the frames per minute set on zero.
And I did not take the LTC photo target 16; I figured that Gordon had implied that it might be a waste of film to shoot them this way; I thought maybe we could get it fixed, we could save the film.
I did shoot the landing because I figured, even if it is working - that one we wouldn't have another chance at.
And I fired 39 frames off of magazine B on the landing per the pad.
I don't know whether it will turn out or not, because it - it clanked and fluttered the whole time.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: One thing I might ask you there.
Did you try trying the power switch On and Off any time when it was clicking away there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I cycled the nonessential Bus switch while it was running.
Saw no change at all from Main A to Main B.

And once you apply the power, it - it goes into its MAG and does its thing there, regardless of whether you have the Mode switch in Auto, Standby, or Single, and whether or not you turn the Power On and Off again.
Generally: now, when I came up to shoot the landing, when I turned the Power switch On a minute before the T start, it clanked all the time but didn't fire off any frames.
And during the - minute plus a few seconds path there, it just kicked one, going off as it should every -one every 2 seconds.
So the thing's kind of random; I can't seem to get a pattern on it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And it still acts like - we got the thing to foul up by a low dc voltage on the thing down here on the ground.
And, like you say, if it's got the voltage up on the thing, it seems to work.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Hey, along that line, Gordon implied that low DC voltage ought to have a flickering Power On light; and that's not true.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, Stu, I've got a bunch of P24 PADs here for you.
Better start copying them, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Hey, let me fire off this 52, and then I'll be right with you.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Ron, let's go to work on the PADs.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: How about trying for the High Gain, Manuel and Medium, Pitch of minus of 73, Yaw of 11.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: When you get it, start with RP-3 on page 13.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Can you read me, Ron; the signal strength doesn't look too good.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The yaw - try a yaw of 25 degree.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Yes, I've still got you here.
Let me play with it just a second.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, Ron.- That looks like about the best I'm going to do.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I think it looks pretty good down here, now.
And ready to start on RP-3.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The P24 tracking PAD for target RP-3 is interpreted as follows:T-1:109:57:54 GET.
This is the time when the landmark will be on the horizon.
It is not the time it will be visible through the sextant optics.T-2:110:02:44 GET.
This is the time when the target can be acquired in the sextant and is when the CSM begins to pitch down.TCA:110:04:24 GET.
This is when the CSM will be closest to the target.T-3:110:05:12 GET.

This is the time when the target will fall out of the sextant's field of view.Note that at the time of the closest approach, Kitty Hawk will not be directly overhead of RP-3, but will be 5 nautical miles to the north.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Let's run them all through, and then I'll read them back.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The P24 tracking PAD for target RP-5 is interpreted as follows:T-1:110:08:45 GET.
This is the time when the landmark will be on the horizon.
It is not the time it will be visible through the sextant optics.T-2:110:13:35 GET.
This is the time when the target can be acquired in the sextant and is when the CSM begins to pitch down.TCA:110:15:15 GET.
This is when the CSM will be closest to the target.T-3:110:16:03 GET.
This is the time when the target will fall out of the sextant's field of view.Note that at the time of the closest approach, Kitty Hawk will pass directly overhead of RP-5.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The P24 tracking PAD for target Daguerre 66 is interpreted as follows:T-1:110:30:11 GET.
This is the time when the landmark will be on the horizon.
It is not the time it will be visible through the sextant optics.T-2:110:35:01 GET.
This is the time when the target can be acquired in the sextant and is when the CSM begins to pitch down.TCA:110:36:41 GET.
This is when the CSM will be closest to the target.T-3:110:37:29 GET.

This is the time when the target will fall out of the sextant's field of view.Note that at the time of the closest approach, Kitty Hawk will not be directly overhead of RP-3, but will be 22 nautical miles to the north.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's north 6, and, of course, it'll be 14-1 for the Noun 89.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You broke up on TCA and T3.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The P24 tracking PAD for target 14-1 is interpreted as follows:T-1:110:46:15 GET.
This is the time when the landmark will be on the horizon.
It is not the time it will be visible through the sextant optics.T-2:110:51:05 GET.
This is the time when the target can be acquired in the sextant and is when the CSM begins to pitch down.TCA:110:52:45 GET.
This is when the CSM will be closest to the target.T-3:110:53:33 GET.

This is the time when the target will fall out of the sextant's field of view.Note that at the time of the closest approach, Kitty Hawk will not be directly overhead of RP-3, but will be 6 nautical miles to the north.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Request P00 and Accept there, and we'll give you a state vector.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Got a map update for the REV 15 on page 13.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: About 45 seconds to LOS; we'll see you on the other side.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is Apollo Control, 109 hours 24 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We have had Loss of Signal with the Command Module Kitty Hawk.
As it passed behind the Moon on the - toward the end of the 14 lunar revolution.
We're still up live for any further conversation between Shepard and Mitchell aboard Antares at the Fra Mauro landing site.

At the present time, they're busy with going through their lunar surface checklist, doing some navigation alignments with the Optical Alignment Telescope which is roughly analogous to the sextant on the Command Module.
Then at 110 hours, 20 minutes, they're scheduled to have a meal.
Some 2 hours from now roughly, they should begin preparations for the first extravehicular activity.

To recapitulate again, the landing phase earlier in the revolution prior to the one in which the power descent and landing was made, some spurious numbers came up in the Lunar Module computer programs which were indications of an abort signal.
These were reckoned to be contamination in the abort switch itself.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Control to continue the recapitulation of the problem with the Lunar Module computer.
The suspected cause was contamination in the abort switch.
By tapping on the switch, the numbers would disappear both in the onboard display keyboard readouts and the telemetered numbers here on the ground.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: To bypass this potential problem, which would cause an abort during the power descent, a computer routine was devised by the manufacturers or the designers, I should say, of the Apollo guidance system whereby the abort signal would be bypassed.
This routine was entered into the DSKY after ignition for power descent and apparently caused no problems with the fairly nominal descent profile and touchdown right on target.
Powered Descent Ignition again, 108:42:29; touchdown at 108:55:14 for a total time in the powered descent of 12 minutes, 46 seconds.
Continuing to monitor the air-ground circuit with Antares on the lunar surface.
At 109 hours, 30 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 109 hours 35 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.

We're estimating a Change of Shift Briefing with the offgoing Flight Director Gerry Griffin and hopefully, spacecraft communicator Fred Haise, over in the small briefing room in the Apollo News Center Houston in about 15 or 20 minutes.
Still up live for any conversations with Lunar Module Antares, this is Apollo Control at 109:36.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: At 109 hours 53 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We've had a handover of flight control teams in Mission Control.
Flight director Pete Frank presently pooling his flight control team for a GO-NO/GO for powerdown.
We'll stand by and continue to monitor.
We're at 109 hours 54 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Most of the onboard systems will be powered down while on the lunar surface, to save consumables.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 110 hours 1 minute Ground Elapsed Time.
The change of shift to news conference is ready to begin in the large auditorium of Building 1, and at this time we will take down the live air to ground and switch to the news conference.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: About time to start the camera on RP-5e?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Well, how are the troops in Antares making out?
Do we have an estimate on the landing spot yet, Ron?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: They're pressing on with all their checks down there.
And everything is still real good.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Did they come right in on the first pick?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Are they right on the target?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: From what we can tell they just passed over the north crater of Triplet, and on down the stream just a little bit, but Just a little bit short of the target, I think, initially.
They haven't started the - their descriptions yet, though.
I haven't gotten the word yet on exactly where they are.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Hey, Ron, got any word on how the low altitude track on 14-1 and high altitude compare?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Let me see if I can get that, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You're kind of down in the mud, Stu.
But your high altitude tracking was excellent, and we've changed the landing site from a previous estimate there, 1400 feet in latitude, and zero in longitude.
We've only got three real-time marks on the low altitude, but it compares real favorably with the high altitude stuff, so far.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Well, now the other marks, on the low altitude, Ron, on the - on the tape, that right?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: They were on the tape, and it took us awhile to get that dumped on the tape, and then - so we're just now getting the tape data.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You've got about 30 seconds to T1time for Daguerre 66.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You're coming up on the T1time.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Time to start your camera there, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And when you finish up there as you're - whipping on across the landing site, those guys are so close to it, you probably can?t tell the difference from up there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Ron, I tried to change the camera and put the sextant on and take a look at - the site; Cone Crater went by just as I got the sextant up.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Couldn't understand what you said that time, Stu.
Just for your information though, Antares is locked onto you with her radar.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: What I said was, that soon as I finished up on 14-1, I tried to get the sextant in, in time to look at the LM; but, by the time I got it in, Cone Crater was just passing out of the field of view.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We'll catch them one of these other REVs.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We'll stand by for the magazine percentage there after - on the landmark tracking magazine.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: And once you get in attitude, we'll use this high gain procedures: Manuel and Medium Beam width.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Request Omni Alpha until we get into the attitude.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Roger, Kitty Hawk; loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: I've got some map updates, and your zero phase PADs for you.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Okay, your zero phase PAD to backward.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Roll, 197-4; pitch, 001.4; yaw, all zips; T-start, 111:59:43.
And make a note there that your switch time will be 8:07- Over.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: And on page 18 now, your zero phase PAD forward.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Roll, 344.7; pitch, 240.1; yaw, all zips; T-start, 112:36:00.
And make a note there that your new switch time is 9:58.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: And just a reminder to be sure and get it on your tape up there.
The verbal marks at your first and last intervalometer actuation.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Just a reminder to be sure and get it on your tape up there - the verbal at your first and last intervalometer actuation.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Okay, we're about to LOS here, Stu.
Get some good gegenschein photos when you go around the corner there.
If you want to write these down and stick them on your site map there, it looks like they're located - located at Charlie Quebec, 0.5 and 65.4.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: This new window shade sure looks great, Ron.
And fits on - back on the camera real good.
Just to be doubly safe, I just got through taping floodlight - right up by the window.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Is somebody thinking up any words about our Descartes photography.
Does it look like the Hycon's wiped out, or we going to do the COAS or -any decision on that?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We're working on it right now, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston at 111 hours 21 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
You heard that long exchange between capsule communicator Bruce McCandless here in Mission Control and the crew of Antares speaking from the Fra Mauro region.
Earlier flight dynamics had given a preliminary set of coordinates of 3.65 South, 17.46 West.
At any case, quite close and the computer on Antares is probably smarter than maps prepared here on Earth.
From that description, Antares appears to be within several feet of the prescribed landing point.
As we look over copies of the onboard grid maps of the area.
Coordinate numbers, by the way, will be further refined with the rendezvous radar tracking.
That's program- computer program 22 on board the LM.
You heard the description of that as Kitty Hawk passes overhead.
Al Shepard, Ed Mitchell obviously well-schooled in their procedures starting first with a general description of geological features and then proceeding with increasing detail.
We're at 111 hours 23 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and standing by continuing to monitor, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at a Ground Elapsed Time of 111 hours 32 minutes into the flight of Apollo 14.

Although most of our attention has been directed toward Antares, there is another flight control team in the mission control that's headed by Flight Director, Glynn Lunney with Ron Evans serving as capsule communicator in contact with Stu Roosa aboard Kitty Hawk.
Presently Kitty Hawk is passing behind the Moon on its - on the 15th revolution.
We will reacquire the Command Module at - in some 34 minutes, we're at 111 hours 33 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You ought to be switching to your second target.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You ought to be switching to your second target.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: How was good old target 2, there?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Well, I'm afraid you're right, Ron.
It was pretty tough, I never saw B at all and A - I think - is pretty far down.
I was pretty surprised at target 1, generally, the targets, A, B, and C are very easy to pick up; but in zero phase, B was Just completely wiped out.
I think the clue is heading down the flat - if you have got some relief by you, even in zero phase, you know, you can sort of use that as - to help you on in.
But in zero phase, B was pretty well bombed out.
D was actually the best of the three and if you can pretty well hang on in, you can pick it up, if you use the other craters as a lead in and A was about - he was -...
and during zero phase that whole ...
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We are getting too much background noise here.
I really couldn't understand anything you said there.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Well, why didn't you stop me?
get it all on the tape - I'll put this other ...
Well, I guess it'll have to be running.
Are you going to leave the tape running during this next ...?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Why don't you go ahead and leave the tape running here.
We sure can't make out what you're saying down here.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: out; maybe we ought to get rid of some of the banging.
How do you hear me now?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Well, something that's supposed to cut out part of the static.
It does cut down on the static, but everytime the signal strength gets a little bit low, it also bangs.
I took those out and now I can hear you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, you've always been loud and clear here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, Ron; are you going to let the tape run for these - during this next sighting?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The comm is a little bit marginal down here, but what we would like to do is leave the tape running until after this next zero phase, but also talk to the ground if you will and then maybe we will have some comments back for you.
And then we should get it in one place or the other for sure.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You want me to terminate the charge on B and start one on Able?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: You've got about 30 seconds to start time.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: About 30 seconds to turn the camera on.
Give me a hack when you turn it on.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You can start on STOP on that one, and switch to target area 4.
We can't hear a thing you're saying down here, but keep talking - We'll get it on the tape.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Will you try to pass down the frame number off of the zero phase stuff?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I've got your map update for REV IT and the zodiacal light photo PAD.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Again, I could just barely read you; but your map update for REV IT: 180, 113:44:40.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, zodiacal light photo PAD T-start, 113:18:03.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I guess our comm didn't hold out through that, did it, Ron?
Seemed like I heard you - heard it drop out.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: As you know, when you come around the other side here, we?re going to want to dump the DSE to get your zero-phase stuff.
So, if you'll come over the hill in these attitudes - You ready to copy?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Hey, I noticed the a - the tape was barber-poled.
I don't know if it went during the second - or not - zero phase; did you stop the tape after the second zero-phase pass?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: That's affirm; we stopped it and rewound it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, that's what I was afraid of.
I was afraid it might have run down - okay, thank you.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Your High Gain angles will be Pitch, plus 33; Yaw of 279.
And you can start the maneuver to that attitude after you complete your zodiacal light stuff.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, I'll start a maneuver after the zodiacal light of 060, 304, 000.
Set up the High Gain on a Pitch of plus 33, Yaw of 279-
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Roger; and, as you can know, that's just 60 degrees out and roll from the LM visual attitude.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Stu; I gave you some bum dope there.
We just stopped the tape at the end of the zero-phase stuff; we did not rewind it, so you can go ahead and use it on the back side.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Your comm's good now; I don't know why it wasn't real good there during zero phase.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Yes; well, I got you on the High Gain now.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Just for your information, EVA preparation seems to be progressing normally down there at the Fra Mauro area.
And they'll probably be out on the surface when you come around the other side.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Did they say anything about the terrain, Ron?
It seems like I heard them say they were - something like an 8-degree roll angle or something.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And it's a little rougher than what they thought it was going to be down there.
That was just a general comment on the terrain.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: They comment on how steep it looked - to get up the Cone?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Hey, Stu; Cone's about 5 o'clock, and they really weren't looking at the slope as they came down across there (laughter).
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Stu; you got about 30 seconds to T-start time.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Stu; we'll see you on the other side.
transcript.speaker: Evans
